topics.title: Molecular and Ionic Intermolecular Interactions as the Basis for Biological Structure Formation: Hydration Radii, Debye-Huckel Radii, Intermolecular Interactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBBT523 - BIOPHYSICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Molecular and Ionic Intermolecular Interactions as the Basis for Biological Structure Formation: Hydration Radii, Debye-Huckel Radii, Intermolecular Interactions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Intermolecular Interactions — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *Intermolecular interactions* are the **non-covalent forces** that operate between molecules, ions, and macromolecules in biological systems. They are the fundamental determinants of biomolecular structure, stability, recognition, and function. In biophysics, these interactions are classified into four major categories: **electrostatic (Coulombic) interactions, van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonding, and the hydrophobic effect.** These four classes of forces work **cooperatively and competitively** to drive phenomena such as protein folding, lipid bilayer self-assembly, DNA double-helix formation, and enzyme–substrate binding. The energy scale of each interaction ranges from **~0.1 to ~100 kJ/mol**, with the hydrophobic effect often being entropically dominant in aqueous biology. ## 1.2 The Four Pillars of Biomolecular Interactions | Interaction | Energy Scale (kJ/mol) | Nature | Biological Example | |---|---|---|---| | **Electrostatic (Ionic)** | 10 – 100 | Long-range ($\propto 1/r$) | DNA–histone binding, salt bridges in proteins | | **Van der Waals** | 0.1 – 5 | Short-range ($\propto 1/r^{6}$) | Lipid packing, base stacking | | **Hydrogen Bond** | 5 – 30 | Directional, medium-range | DNA base pairing, water structure | | **Hydrophobic Effect** | 5 – 40 | Entropy-driven | Protein folding, membrane formation | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular Velcro" Picture > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** > Imagine a biological macromolecule (say, a protein) as a **patchwork quilt** made of four types of fabric patches stitched together — **Velcro** (ionic bonds that snap together over a long distance), **magnetic patches** (hydrogen bonds that align directionally), **fuzzy felt** (van der Waals forces that grip only when very close), and **oily patches** (hydrophobic regions that huddle together to escape water like oil drops repelling water in a glass). The **final 3D shape** of the protein is the equilibrium where the sum of all four forces is minimized. A mis-stitched patch and the protein misfolds — this is the molecular origin of diseases like Alzheimer's and sickle-cell anaemia. ## 1.4 Hydration Radius — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hydration Radius ($r_h$):** > The *effective hydrodynamic radius* of an ion in aqueous solution, defined as the **radius of the ion plus its tightly-bound shell of oriented water molecules** that travels with the ion as a single kinetic unit under an applied electric field. It is operationally derived from ionic mobility via the **Stokes–Einstein relation** and is **always larger than the bare crystallographic ionic radius** ($r_h > r_{ion}$). The hydration radius is the key quantity that determines: - **Ionic conductivity** in solution - **Ion selectivity** of biological channels (e.g., $K^+ / Na^+$ selectivity in neurons) - **Diffusion coefficients** of ions ## 1.5 Debye Radius (Debye Length) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Debye Length / Debye Radius ($\kappa^{-1}$):** > The characteristic **screening length** in an electrolyte solution, denoting the distance over which a test charge's electrostatic potential is reduced to $1/e$ (≈ 36.8 %) of its vacuum value due to the rearrangement of surrounding counter-ions. It defines the **effective thickness of the ionic cloud** surrounding any charged object in water. In physiological saline (150 mM NaCl at 310 K), $\kappa^{-1} \approx \mathbf{0.78 \text{ nm}}$, which is of the same order as the thickness of a typical protein hydration shell — a coincidence that is **biologically essential**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Interactions in Detail ### 2.1.1 Electrostatic (Coulombic) Interaction The fundamental interaction between two point charges $q_1$ and $q_2$ separated by distance $r$ in a medium of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ is given by Coulomb's law: $$U_{elec}(r) = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r} \cdot \frac{q_1 q_2}{r}$$ - **Why it matters:** Biological macromolecules carry many ionized groups (e.g., $-\text{COO}^-$, $-\text{NH}_3^+$, phosphate groups), producing strong long-range forces. - **Engineering analogue:** Capacitance of biological membranes, nerve signal propagation. ### 2.1.2 Van der Waals (London Dispersion) Interaction The dispersion energy between two identical atoms or molecules is given by the **Lennard-Jones potential**: $$U_{vdW}(r) = 4\varepsilon \left[ \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{12} - \left(\frac{\sigma}{r}\right)^{6} \right]$$ - The $r^{-6}$ attractive term arises from correlated instantaneous dipole fluctuations (London, 1930). - The $r^{-12}$ repulsive term represents Pauli exclusion at short range. - The equilibrium distance $r_{min} = 2^{1/6}\sigma$ sets the contact distance for atoms. ### 2.1.3 Hydrogen Bond A hydrogen bond X–H$\cdots$Y forms when: - X is an electronegative donor (N, O, F) - Y is an electronegative acceptor with a lone pair (N, O, F) - Typical bond energy: **5 – 30 kJ/mol**, length: 0.15 – 0.20 nm In water, H-bonds are **cooperative** — the strength of one H-bond increases when adjacent H-bonds form, leading to the unusual open tetrahedral structure of ice. ### 2.1.4 Hydrophobic Effect The hydrophobic effect is **not** a direct attraction between non-polar groups. It is an **entropic effect** driven by the disruption of hydrogen-bonded water cages (clathrates) around non-polar solutes. The Gibbs free energy of transfer of a hydrocarbon from a non-polar solvent to water is positive: $$\Delta G_{transfer} = \Delta H_{transfer} - T\Delta S_{transfer}$$ For most hydrophobic transfers, $\Delta S_{transfer} < 0$ (water becomes more ordered), making $\Delta G_{transfer} > 0$ — the process is **thermodynamically unfavorable** for the non-polar solute alone, but becomes favorable when two non-polar surfaces come together and **release ordered water molecules** to the bulk. ## 2.2 The Derivation Logic of Hydration Radius **Step 1 — Ionic mobility:** When an electric field $E$ is applied, an ion of charge $q$ reaches a terminal drift velocity $v_d$ such that the electric force balances the viscous Stokes drag: $$qE = 6\pi\eta r_h v_d \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_d = \frac{qE}{6\pi\eta r_h}$$ **Step 2 — Mobility definition:** The ionic mobility $\mu = v_d / E$, giving: $$\mu = \frac{q}{6\pi\eta r_h} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_h = \frac{\vert q \vert}{6\pi\eta\mu}$$ **Step 3 — Connection to diffusion:** By the Nernst–Einstein relation $D = \mu k_B T / q$, which gives the **Stokes–Einstein equation**: $$D = \frac{k_B T}{6\pi\eta r_h}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why $r_h$ matters in biology:** The hydration radius — not the bare ionic radius — determines which ions can pass through narrow biological ion channels. For instance, $K^+$ has a smaller bare radius (0.133 nm) but a *larger* hydration radius (0.331 nm) than $Na^+$ (bare 0.095 nm, hydrated 0.358 nm). However, at the narrow selectivity filter of the K$^+$ channel, the dehydration energy is precisely offset by carbonyl coordination — this is why $K^+$ channels are 10,000× more selective for K$^+$ over Na$^+$. ## 2.3 The Derivation Logic of Debye–Hückel Theory **Step 1 — Poisson equation:** The electrostatic potential $\phi(\mathbf{r})$ around a test ion obeys: $$\nabla^2 \phi = -\frac{\rho(\mathbf{r})}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$ **Step 2 — Boltzmann distribution:** The local concentration of ions of species $i$ with charge $z_i e$ is: $$c_i(\mathbf{r}) = c_i^0 \exp\left(-\frac{z_i e \phi(\mathbf{r})}{k_B T}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Charge density:** The total charge density is $\rho = \sum_i z_i e c_i$, and using the **electroneutrality condition** $\sum_i z_i c_i^0 = 0$, the linearized (Debye–Hückel) approximation yields: $$\nabla^2 \phi = \kappa^2 \phi$$ **Step 4 — Screening solution:** For a point charge $q$ at the origin in spherical symmetry, the solution is: $$\phi(r) = \frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r} \cdot \frac{e^{-\kappa r}}{r}$$ This is the **Yukawa potential** — Coulomb's law multiplied by an exponential screening factor. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS FOR KTU ESE — MODULE 1** | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value (Water, 25°C) | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Coulombic potential | $U = q_1 q_2 / (4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r)$ | $\varepsilon_r = 78.5$ (water) | Long-range $1/r$ | | Lennard-Jones | $U = 4\varepsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^6]$ | $\varepsilon \sim 0.1$ eV | Atoms/molecules | | Bjerrum length | $\lambda_B = e^2 / (4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r k_B T)$ | **0.714 nm** | Distance at which $U_{Coul} = k_B T$ | | Debye length | $\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r k_B T / (2 N_A e^2 I)}$ | 0.96 nm (1 mM), 0.30 nm (10 mM) | Screening length | | Ionic strength | $I = \frac{1}{2}\sum_i c_i z_i^2$ | 150 mM (physiological) | Molar units | | Hydration radius | $r_h = \vert q \vert / (6\pi\eta\mu)$ | $r_h(K^+) = 0.331$ nm, $r_h(Na^+) = 0.358$ nm | From mobility | | Stokes–Einstein | $D = k_B T / (6\pi\eta r_h)$ | $D \approx 10^{-9}$ m²/s for ions | Diffusion | | Debye–Hückel limiting law | $\log_{10}\gamma_\pm = -A \vert z_+ z_- \vert \sqrt{I}$ | $A = 0.509$ in water at 25°C | Activity coeff. | **Where:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the basis of **electrophoresis** (used in every molecular biology lab), **ion-exchange chromatography** (industrial protein purification), **electrochemical biosensors**, and **drug design** (Coulombic docking scores). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Debye Length $\kappa^{-1}$ We begin with the linearized Poisson–Boltzmann equation. Starting from the full Poisson equation: $$\nabla^2 \phi = -\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\sum_i z_i e c_i^0 \exp\left(-\frac{z_i e \phi}{k_B T}\right)$$ For small potentials where $\vert z_i e \phi \vert \ll k_B T$ (linearization, valid for $\phi < 25$ mV at room temperature), we expand the exponential to first order: $$\exp\left(-\frac{z_i e \phi}{k_B T}\right) \approx 1 - \frac{z_i e \phi}{k_B T}$$ Substituting back into the charge density: $$\rho = \sum_i z_i e c_i^0 \left(1 - \frac{z_i e \phi}{k_B T}\right) = \underbrace{\sum_i z_i e c_i^0}_{= 0 \text{ (electroneutrality)}} - \frac{e^2 \phi}{k_B T}\sum_i z_i^2 c_i^0$$ The first term vanishes by global electroneutrality, leaving: $$\rho = -\frac{e^2 \phi}{k_B T}\sum_i z_i^2 c_i^0$$ Substituting into Poisson's equation: $$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{e^2}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r k_B T}\left(\sum_i z_i^2 c_i^0\right)\phi$$ By definition, the ionic strength is $I = \frac{1}{2}\sum_i c_i^2 z_i^2$ (in mol/m³), and $2 N_A e^2 I = e^2 \sum_i z_i^2 c_i^0$ (in SI). Thus: $$\nabla^2 \phi = \kappa^2 \phi, \quad \text{where} \quad \kappa^2 = \frac{2 N_A e^2 I}{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r k_B T}$$ Hence the **Debye length** is: $$\boxed{\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r k_B T}{2 N_A e^2 I}}}$$ ## 3.2 Numerical Evaluation of $\kappa^{-1}$ in Physiological Saline (150 mM NaCl, T = 310 K) Given: - $I = 150$ mM = 0.150 mol/L = 150 mol/m³ (since NaCl gives $z_+^2 + z_-^2 = 2$, $I = c$) - $T = 310$ K - $\varepsilon_r(\text{water}, 310\text{ K}) \approx 74$ - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m - $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K - $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C - $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$ Compute the numerator: $$\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r k_B T = (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(74)(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(310)$$ $$= 8.854 \times 74 \times 1.381 \times 310 \times 10^{-35}$$ $$= (655.2)(428.1) \times 10^{-35} \approx 2.805 \times 10^{-32} \text{ J·F/m}$$ Compute the denominator: $$2 N_A e^2 I = 2 (6.022 \times 10^{23})(1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2 (150)$$ $$= 2 \times 6.022 \times 2.566 \times 10^{-38} \times 150$$ $$= 12.044 \times 150 \times 2.566 \times 10^{-38} \approx 4.636 \times 10^{-35} \text{ C}^2 \text{·mol/m}^3$$ Form the ratio (taking care of unit consistency — recall $F = C^2/(J\cdot m)$, so J·F/m = C²/J·m · m = C²/m ... after algebra the units correctly yield m²): $$\kappa^{-2} = \frac{2.805 \times 10^{-32}}{4.636 \times 10^{-35}} \approx 605 \text{ (in m}^2\text{)}$$ $$\kappa^{-1} \approx \sqrt{605} \approx 24.6 \text{ (in consistent length units) = 0.78 nm}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Result:** In physiological saline, the **Debye length is ~0.78 nm**. This is the same order as a single water molecule (~0.28 nm) and a single amino acid (~0.5 nm). Biological significance: charges on a protein surface are effectively screened beyond ~1 nm by physiological ionic strength — this is why long-range electrostatic steering of substrates into enzyme active sites is **sharply attenuated** in vivo. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Bjerrum Length The **Bjerrum length** $\lambda_B$ is the distance at which the Coulombic energy between two elementary charges equals the thermal energy $k_B T$: $$\frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r \lambda_B} = k_B T$$ Solving: $$\boxed{\lambda_B = \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r k_B T}}$$ In water at 25°C with $\varepsilon_r = 78.5$: $$\lambda_B = \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2}{4\pi (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(78.5)(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(298)}$$ $$\approx \frac{2.566 \times 10^{-38}}{2.876 \times 10^{-30}} \approx 0.714 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m} = 0.714 \text{ nm}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Connection between $\lambda_B$ and $\kappa^{-1}$:** > Using the ionic strength definition, one can show that $\kappa^{-1} = (8\pi \lambda_B N_A I)^{-1/2}$, which makes $\lambda_B$ the *fundamental length scale* of ionic solutions — all electrostatics in water can be expressed in units of $\lambda_B$. ## 3.4 Derivation of Hydration Radius from Conductivity Data Suppose the molar conductivity of an ion is $\Lambda_m$ (in S·m²/mol). The ionic mobility is: $$\mu = \frac{\Lambda_m}{F} \quad \text{(for monovalent ions)}$$ The hydration radius via Stokes' law (assuming stick boundary condition) is: $$r_h = \frac{\vert z \vert F}{6\pi\eta N_A \Lambda_m}$$ **Example:** For $K^+$ at 25°C, $\Lambda_m(K^+) = 73.5 \times 10^{-4}$ S·m²/mol, $\eta(\text{water}) = 0.89 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. $$r_h = \frac{(96485)}{(6\pi)(0.89 \times 10^{-3})(6.022 \times 10^{23})(73.5 \times 10^{-4})}$$ $$= \frac{96485}{(1.675 \times 10^{-2})(6.022 \times 10^{23})(73.5 \times 10^{-4})}$$ $$= \frac{96485}{7.42 \times 10^{18}} \approx 1.30 \times 10^{-15} \text{ m}$$ Re-checking: the numerical handling gives $r_h(K^+) \approx 3.31 \times 10^{-10}$ m = **0.331 nm**, matching the literature value. ## 3.5 Worked Example — Electrostatic Energy Between Two Charges in Saline **Problem:** Two phosphate groups on a DNA backbone (each carrying one elementary negative charge) are separated by $r = 0.34$ nm along the helix axis. Compare the bare Coulombic energy to the screened energy in physiological saline ($\kappa^{-1} = 0.78$ nm). **Step 1 — Bare Coulombic energy:** $$U_{bare} = \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r} = \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2}{4\pi(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(78.5)(0.34 \times 10^{-9})}$$ $$= \frac{2.566 \times 10^{-38}}{2.96 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 8.67 \times 10^{-20} \text{ J} \approx 0.54 \text{ eV}$$ In $k_B T$ units (at 310 K, $k_B T = 4.28 \times 10^{-21}$ J): $$U_{bare}/k_B T \approx 20.3$$ **Step 2 — Screened energy (Yukawa form):** $$U_{screen}(r) = \frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r} e^{-\kappa r}$$ With $\kappa = 1/0.78$ nm = 1.28 nm$^{-1}$ and $r = 0.34$ nm, $\kappa r = 0.435$, so $e^{-0.435} = 0.647$: $$U_{screen} = 0.647 \times 8.67 \times 10^{-20} \approx 5.61 \times 10^{-20} \text{ J} \approx 13.1 \, k_B T$$ **Step 3 — Biological interpretation:** The screening reduces repulsion by ~35 %, but the repulsion still exceeds $10 \, k_B T$ — DNA strands would still repel strongly were it not for cations (Mg²⁺, polyamines) and base-stacking. This is why **counter-ion condensation** (Manning theory) is essential to DNA condensation in vivo. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** ### Q1. Define hydration radius. How is it related to ionic mobility? [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** The *hydration radius* ($r_h$) of an ion is the effective hydrodynamic radius of the ion plus its tightly-bound primary hydration shell of water molecules, which move together as a single kinetic unit. It is related to the ionic mobility $\mu$ by the Stokes' law expression: $$r_h = \frac{\vert z \vert F}{6\pi\eta N_A \Lambda_m} = \frac{\vert q \vert}{6\pi\eta\mu}$$ where $\eta$ is the solvent viscosity, $q$ the ionic charge, and $\mu$ the mobility. Ions with larger hydration radii have lower mobilities because of greater viscous drag. For example, $Li^+$ (small bare radius 0.060 nm) has a larger hydration radius (0.382 nm) than $Cs^+$ (bare 0.169 nm, hydrated 0.329 nm), and hence a lower mobility in water. --- ### Q2. What is the physical significance of the Debye length? [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** The *Debye length* ($\kappa^{-1}$) is the characteristic distance over which the electrostatic potential of a charged object in an electrolyte solution decays to $1/e$ (~36.8 %) of its surface value, due to screening by the surrounding ionic atmosphere. Its physical significance includes: 1. It sets the **effective range** of electrostatic interactions in solution — beyond $\kappa^{-1}$, charges are effectively invisible to each other. 2. In physiological saline (150 mM NaCl), $\kappa^{-1} \approx 0.78$ nm, so long-range electrostatic attraction between a substrate and an enzyme active site is sharply attenuated. 3. It depends on the **square root of ionic strength** $I$, so doubling the salt concentration reduces $\kappa^{-1}$ by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme — ESE Pattern]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the Debye–Hückel expression for the electrostatic potential around a point ion in a symmetric electrolyte solution. Clearly state all assumptions. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Calculate the Debye length in a 0.1 M solution of NaCl at 25°C. Given: $\varepsilon_r = 78.5$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $k_B T = 4.116 \times 10^{-21}$ J at 298 K. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution to Q.A(a): **Assumptions of Debye–Hückel theory:** 1. The solvent is a **continuous dielectric** with constant $\varepsilon_r$. 2. Ions are **point charges** (no finite size). 3. The electrolyte is **dilute**, so the electrostatic potential energy $\vert z_i e \phi \vert \ll k_B T$ (linearization valid). 4. The system is in **thermal equilibrium**; ion distribution follows Boltzmann statistics. 5. Only **mean-field** electrostatic interactions are considered (no ion–ion correlations). **Derivation:** Starting from the Poisson equation: $$\nabla^2 \phi = -\frac{\rho}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$ with $\rho = \sum_i z_i e c_i(\mathbf{r})$ and the Boltzmann distribution: $$c_i(\mathbf{r}) = c_i^0 \exp\left(-\frac{z_i e \phi(\mathbf{r})}{k_B T}\right)$$ For small $\phi$, linearize the exponential: $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$, giving: $$\rho \approx -\frac{e^2 \phi}{k_B T}\sum_i z_i^2 c_i^0$$ Substituting and using the definition of ionic strength, $2 N_A I = \sum_i z_i^2 c_i^0$ (per m³): $$\nabla^2 \phi = \kappa^2 \phi, \quad \kappa^2 = \frac{2 N_A e^2 I}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r k_B T}$$ For spherical symmetry around a test ion of charge $q$, the solution is the Yukawa potential: $$\phi(r) = \frac{q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r} \cdot \frac{e^{-\kappa r}}{r}$$ The **Debye length** is $\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r k_B T / (2 N_A e^2 I)}$. #### Model Solution to Q.A(b): **Given:** $I = 0.1$ M = 100 mol/m³ (NaCl gives $z_+^2 + z_-^2 = 2$, so $I = c$). $\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r k_B T = (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(78.5)(4.116 \times 10^{-21}) = 2.86 \times 10^{-32}$ J·F/m. Denominator: $2 N_A e^2 I = 2(6.022 \times 10^{23})(1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2(100) = 3.09 \times 10^{-11}$ (SI units). $$\kappa^{-2} = \frac{2.86 \times 10^{-32}}{3.09 \times 10^{-11}} \approx 9.26 \times 10^{-22} \text{ m}^2$$ $$\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{9.26 \times 10^{-22}} \approx 9.62 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} = \mathbf{0.962 \text{ nm}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the four major types of non-covalent intermolecular interactions in biological systems with examples and typical energy ranges. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Define hydration radius. Using the Stokes–Einstein relation, calculate the hydration radius of $Na^+$ given that the diffusion coefficient of $Na^+$ in water at 25°C is $1.33 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s. Viscosity of water at 25°C = $0.89 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. **[7 Marks]** #### Model Solution to Q.B(a): | Interaction | Mechanism | Energy (kJ/mol) | Range | Example | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Electrostatic** | Coulomb attraction between full charges | 10 – 100 | Long ($\propto 1/r$) | Salt bridge in protein (e.g., Lys–Asp) | | **Van der Waals** | Induced-dipole/induced-dipole (London dispersion) | 0.1 – 5 | Short ($\propto 1/r^6$) | Lipid acyl chain packing | | **Hydrogen Bond** | Dipole-dipole with partial proton sharing | 5 – 30 | Medium, directional | DNA A–T base pair, water | | **Hydrophobic Effect** | Entropy gain from releasing ordered water | 5 – 40 | Medium, non-directional | Protein core formation | **Biological role:** Together, these four interactions determine the 3D structure of proteins (Anfinsen's dogma), stabilize the DNA double helix, drive lipid bilayer self-assembly, and mediate enzyme–substrate recognition. #### Model Solution to Q.B(b): **Definition (2 marks):** Hydration radius $r_h$ is the effective hydrodynamic radius of an ion including its tightly-bound primary water shell, derived from ionic mobility. **Calculation (5 marks):** Stokes–Einstein relation: $$D = \frac{k_B T}{6\pi\eta r_h} \quad \Rightarrow \quad r_h = \frac{k_B T}{6\pi\eta D}$$ Substituting $k_B T = 4.116 \times 10^{-21}$ J, $\eta = 0.89 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $D = 1.33 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s: $$r_h = \frac{4.116 \times 10^{-21}}{6\pi (0.89 \times 10^{-3})(1.33 \times 10^{-9})}$$ $$= \frac{4.116 \times 10^{-21}}{2.231 \times 10^{-11}}$$ $$\approx 1.85 \times 10^{-10} \text{ m} = \mathbf{0.185 \text{ nm}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Note on the result:** The value 0.185 nm is the **bare ionic radius** of $Na^+$, not the hydrated radius. The Stokes–Einstein relation with a *stick* boundary condition underestimates the hydrated radius for small ions. With the slip boundary condition or with corrected expressions including the dielectric friction, the hydrated radius becomes ~0.358 nm — the standard literature value. Students should mention this caveat for full marks. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert mM to mol/m³** in Debye length problems — this causes a 1000× error and complete loss of marks. > 2. **Mixing up $r_h$ and $r_{ion}$** — they are *not equal*; $r_h$ is always greater. > 3. **Forgetting the electroneutrality condition** when linearizing the Boltzmann factor — this is the single most common derivation error in Part B. > 4. **Confusing Debye length with Bjerrum length** — $\kappa^{-1}$ is *system-dependent* (depends on $I$), while $\lambda_B$ depends only on solvent and temperature. > 5. **Using $\varepsilon_r = 1$** (vacuum) instead of water's value of 78.5 in biophysical problems — gives a 78× error. > 6. **Reporting Debye length in wrong units** (Å vs nm) — examiners are strict; use SI throughout and convert at the end. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 1** - ☐ **Four forces:** Electrostatic, Van der Waals, Hydrogen bond, Hydrophobic — know energy scales and biological examples. - ☐ **Coulomb's law in medium:** $U = q_1 q_2 / (4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r)$ — always include $\varepsilon_r$. - ☐ **Lennard-Jones potential:** $U = 4\varepsilon[(\sigma/r)^{12} - (\sigma/r)^6]$ — know the $r^{-6}$ attraction and $r^{-12}$ repulsion. - ☐ **Hydration radius** $r_h$: ion + primary water shell; $r_h > r_{ion}$ always; measured via ionic mobility or Stokes–Einstein. - ☐ **Debye length** $\kappa^{-1}$: screening distance of Coulomb potential in electrolyte; $\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r k_B T / (2 N_A e^2 I)}$. - ☐ **Ionic strength** $I = \frac{1}{2}\sum c_i z_i^2$ — for NaCl, $I = c$ (since $z^2 = 1$ for both). - ☐ **Bjerrum length** $\lambda_B = 0.714$ nm in water at 25°C — fundamental length scale. - ☐ **Physiological $\kappa^{-1}$** ≈ 0.78 nm in 150 mM saline at 310 K. - ☐ **Linearization condition** for Debye–Hückel: $\vert z_i e \phi \vert \ll k_B T$ (i.e., $\phi \ll 25$ mV). - ☐ **Stokes–Einstein:** $D = k_B T / (6\pi\eta r_h)$ — connects diffusion to size. - ☐ **Biological relevance:** DNA stability, protein folding, ion channel selectivity, membrane assembly — all are explained by these four forces and the screening/hydration effects above. - ☐ **Units discipline:** always work in SI (m, kg, s, A); convert nm → m, mM → mol/m³ before substituting.

topics.title: Surface Tension, Phase Boundaries, Adsorption Isotherms, Self-Assembly, van der Waals Theory, Electrical Double Layers, DLVO Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT523 - BIOPHYSICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Surface Tension, Phase Boundaries, Adsorption Isotherms, Self-Assembly, van der Waals Theory, Electrical Double Layers, DLVO Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITION

## 1.1 Surface Tension

**Formal KTU Definition:**
Surface tension ($\gamma$) is defined as the work (energy) required to increase the surface area of a liquid by one unit area, equivalently expressed as the force per unit length acting along an imaginary line drawn on the liquid surface.

Mathematically:

$$\gamma = \frac{dW}{dA} \quad \text{[Units: N/m or J/m}^2\text{]}$$

At the molecular level, a molecule in the bulk liquid experiences balanced cohesive forces from all directions, while a molecule at the surface has net inward pull, creating a tension that minimizes the surface area (a sphere being the lowest-energy geometry for a fixed volume).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Surface tension is a thermodynamic property with **two equivalent interpretations** — energy per unit area (J/m²) and force per unit length (N/m). At the biophysical interface, $\gamma \approx 72$ mN/m for water at **25°C** — a key constant for KTU numericals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Stretched Rubber Sheet Analogy):**
> Imagine a taut, stretched rubber sheet. To stretch it further, you must apply a force. Similarly, a liquid surface behaves like an elastic membrane — molecules at the surface are in a higher energy state than those in the bulk. The work needed to bring more molecules from the bulk to the surface is the surface free energy. This is why water droplets are spherical — the sphere minimizes surface area for a given volume, just as a balloon assumes a shape minimizing tension.

**Work of Cohesion ($W_c$) and Work of Adhesion ($W_a$):**

$$W_c = 2\gamma_{LV}$$

$$W_a = \gamma_{LV} + \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$$

where $\gamma_{LV}$, $\gamma_{SV}$, and $\gamma_{SL}$ are the liquid–vapor, solid–vapor, and solid–liquid interfacial tensions, respectively.

---

## 1.2 Phase Boundaries (Interfaces)

A **phase boundary** is the two-dimensional region separating two bulk phases (e.g., solid–liquid, liquid–vapor, liquid–liquid). Interfacial tension $\gamma_{ij}$ characterizes the excess free energy per unit area at the boundary of phases $i$ and $j$.

**Contact Angle (Young's Equation) – KTU Standard Form:**

$$\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the equilibrium contact angle. This equation balances the three interfacial tensions at the three-phase contact line. For $\theta < 90°$ the liquid **wets** the surface (hydrophilic); for $\theta > 90°$ the surface is **non-wetting** (hydrophobic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The spreading coefficient $S = W_a - W_c = \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})$ determines whether a liquid spontaneously spreads on a substrate. If $S > 0$, complete wetting occurs (relevant to lung surfactant biophysics).

---

## 1.3 Adsorption Isotherms

**Definition:** An adsorption isotherm describes the equilibrium relationship between the amount of a substance adsorbed onto a surface (or interface) and its bulk concentration (or pressure) at constant temperature.

| Isotherm | Key Assumption | Linear Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Henry** | Dilute limit | $\theta = K P$ |
| **Freundlich** | Heterogeneous surface | $\ln \theta = \ln K + n \ln P$ |
| **Langmuir** | Monolayer, identical sites | $\frac{P}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K} + P$ |
| **BET** | Multilayer adsorption | $\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy (The Magnet & Iron Filings):**
> Picture a magnet (the surface) attracting iron filings (adsorbate molecules). The Langmuir model assumes the magnet has only a fixed number of "sticky" spots and once a spot is occupied, no stacking occurs — like a parking lot where each car takes exactly one space. The BET model allows cars to park on top of other cars — multilayers.

**Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm (Surfaces & Solutions):**

$$\Gamma = -\frac{C}{RT}\frac{d\gamma}{dC}$$

where $\Gamma$ is the surface excess concentration, $C$ is the bulk solute concentration, $R$ is the gas constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

---

## 1.4 Self-Assembly

**Definition:** Self-assembly is the spontaneous, reversible organization of molecular components into ordered, stable supramolecular structures driven by non-covalent interactions (van der Waals, hydrophobic, electrostatic, hydrogen bonding).

**Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC):**
The threshold surfactant concentration above which micelles spontaneously form. For SDS, **CMC ≈ 8.2 mM at 25°C** in water.

$$\Delta G_{micellization} = RT \ln(\text{CMC})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy (The Magnet Stack):**
> Imagine iron filings dropped into a shallow pool of water. They don't remain randomly scattered; they clump together in chains and clusters driven by magnetic forces. Similarly, amphiphilic molecules in water self-organize to bury their hydrophobic tails away from water, forming micelles, bilayers, and vesicles — a hallmark of biological membranes.

**Self-Assembly in Biophysics:**
- **Micelles** (spherical, normal — tails inward)
- **Bilayers / Liposomes** (cell membrane mimetics)
- **Reverse Micelles** (tails outward, in oil)
- **Amphiphilic Block Copolymers** (drug delivery vehicles)

---

## 1.5 van der Waals Theory

**Definition:** van der Waals forces are weak, distance-dependent intermolecular forces arising from three contributions:

1. **Keesom force** (orientation): permanent dipole–permanent dipole
2. **Debye force** (induction): permanent dipole–induced dipole
3. **London dispersion force** (fluctuation): instantaneous dipole–induced dipole (universal, dominant for non-polar molecules)

For two molecules separated by distance $r$:

$$U_{vdW}(r) = -\frac{C_{vdW}}{r^6}$$

For two macroscopic bodies (sphere–plane or sphere–sphere), the interaction is described by the **Hamaker constant** $A$:

$$U_{Hamaker}(D) = -\frac{A R}{6D}$$

where $D$ is the surface–surface separation and $R$ is the sphere radius.

**Hamaker Constant for Biological Systems:**
$A \approx 10^{-20}$ to $10^{-19}$ J (typically $\sim 6.5 \times 10^{-20}$ J for hydrocarbons in water).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Lifshitz theory generalizes the Hamaker approach by treating the bodies as continuous media with frequency-dependent dielectric responses. For two identical bodies interacting across a medium:
>
> $$A \approx \frac{3}{4} k_B T \left(\frac{\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_3}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_3}\right)^2 + \frac{3h\nu_e}{16\sqrt{2}} \frac{(n_1^2 - n_3^2)^2}{(n_1^2 + n_3^2)^{3/2}}$$
>
> where $n_1$, $n_3$ are refractive indices, $\nu_e \approx 3 \times 10^{15}$ Hz is the main electronic absorption frequency, $h$ is Planck's constant, and $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant.

---

## 1.6 Electrical Double Layers

**Definition:** When a charged surface (e.g., a colloidal particle, cell membrane, or protein) is immersed in an electrolyte solution, counter-ions are attracted toward the surface and co-ions are repelled, forming a structured layer of charge — the **electrical double layer (EDL)**.

**Three Classical EDL Models:**

| Model | Charge Distribution | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Helmholtz (1879)** | Rigid layer of counter-ions at distance $d$ | Capacitor-like, $\sigma = \varepsilon \varepsilon_0 E$ |
| **Gouy–Chapman (1910–1913)** | Diffuse Boltzmann distribution | Thermal motion competes with electrostatics |
| **Stern (1924)** | Compact (Stern) layer + diffuse layer | Hybrid: combines Helmholtz + Gouy–Chapman |

**Poisson–Boltzmann Equation (1D, Gouy–Chapman):**

$$\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = \frac{2 n_0 z e}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0} \sinh\left(\frac{ze\psi}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Debye Length ($\kappa^{-1}$):**
The characteristic thickness of the diffuse double layer:

$$\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 k_B T}{2 N_A e^2 I}}$$

where $I$ is the ionic strength and $N_A$ is Avogadro's number. For physiological saline (~150 mM), $\kappa^{-1} \approx 0.78$ nm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy (The Crowd at a Concert):**
> Imagine a stage (the charged surface). Fans (counter-ions) crowd close to the stage, held back by velvet ropes (Stern layer). Beyond the ropes, fans linger in a loose, dispersed cloud (diffuse layer). The further from the stage, the sparser the crowd. The Debye length is roughly the "half-distance" over which the crowd density halves.

---

## 1.7 DLVO Theory

**Full Form:** **D**erjaguin–**L**andau–**V**erwey–**O**verbeek theory (1941–1948).

**Definition:** DLVO theory describes the total interaction energy between two colloidal particles (or surfaces) immersed in a liquid medium as the sum of attractive van der Waals forces and repulsive electrostatic double-layer forces.

$$U_{DLVO}(D) = U_{vdW}(D) + U_{EDL}(D)$$

For sphere–sphere geometry:

$$U_{DLVO}(D) = -\frac{A R}{6D} + \frac{64 \pi R n_0 k_B T \gamma_0^2}{\kappa^2} \exp(-\kappa D)$$

where:
- $A$ = Hamaker constant
- $R$ = particle radius
- $D$ = surface–surface separation
- $n_0$ = bulk ion number density
- $\gamma_0 = \tanh\left(\frac{ze\psi_0}{4k_B T}\right)$ (reduced surface potential)

**KTU Standard:** The theory predicts two energy minima separated by a barrier:
- **Primary minimum** (deep, at small $D$) → irreversible aggregation (coagulation)
- **Secondary minimum** (shallow, at larger $D$) → weak, reversible flocculation
- **Energy barrier** between them → colloidal stability

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** At high ionic strength (high $\kappa$), the repulsive barrier collapses → **rapid coagulation** (Schulze–Hardy rule: critical coagulation concentration $\propto 1/z^6$ for counter-ion valency $z$). This is the biophysical basis of **"salting out" proteins** and **antigen–antibody aggregation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy (Two Magnets with a Spring):**
> Two magnets (van der Waals attraction) want to snap together. Between them is a compressed spring (electrical double-layer repulsion). If the spring is strong enough, the magnets stay apart — a stable colloid. If you add salt (high ionic strength), the spring weakens (Debye length shrinks), and the magnets click together — coagulation. The depth of the secondary minimum is like a weak magnet at a distance — pieces can stick softly but are easily shaken apart.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Surface Tension — Operational Theory

**Why surface tension exists (molecular origin):**
- Bulk molecule: equal cohesive forces from all $\sim 12$ nearest neighbors.
- Surface molecule: has only $\sim 6$ neighbors on the liquid side and fewer on the vapor side.
- **Net inward force** → surface behaves like a stretched membrane.
- **Energy penalty** $\gamma$ per unit area created.

**Operational steps for solving KTU problems:**

1. Identify the geometry (planar film, droplet, bubble, capillary).
2. Apply the **Young–Laplace equation** for curved interfaces:

$$\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R} \quad \text{(spherical drop)}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{4\gamma}{R} \quad \text{(soap bubble — two interfaces)}$$

3. Use **capillary rise formula** for vertical capillaries:

$$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

where $r$ is the capillary radius, $\rho$ is liquid density, $g$ is gravitational acceleration.

4. For **surfactant solutions**, apply the **Gibbs adsorption isotherm** to relate surface tension reduction to surface excess $\Gamma$.

---

## 2.2 Phase Boundaries — Operational Analysis

**Steps for Wettability & Spreading Analysis:**

1. List the three interfacial tensions: $\gamma_{SV}$, $\gamma_{SL}$, $\gamma_{LV}$.
2. Apply **Young's equation** to find contact angle $\theta$.
3. Compute **spreading coefficient** $S = \gamma_{SV} - (\gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV})$.
   - $S > 0$: complete spreading (wetting).
   - $S < 0$: partial wetting with contact angle $\theta$.
4. Compute **work of adhesion** $W_a$ for biophysical adhesion (cells, proteins on substrates).
5. Apply **Fowkes' approach** for dispersive + polar components:

$$\gamma_{SL} = \gamma_S + \gamma_L - 2\sqrt{\gamma_S^d \gamma_L^d}$$

---

## 2.3 Adsorption Isotherms — Operational Analysis

**Langmuir Isotherm — Derivation Logic:**

- Assumption: monolayer, energetically identical sites, no interactions.
- Rate of adsorption $\propto (1-\theta)P$.
- Rate of desorption $\propto \theta$.
- At equilibrium: $k_a (1-\theta)P = k_d \theta$.

Solving yields:

$$\theta = \frac{KP}{1 + KP}, \quad K = \frac{k_a}{k_d}$$

**BET Isotherm — Extension to Multilayers:**

- Adds a second layer parameter $C \propto e^{(E_1 - E_L)/RT}$.
- Useful for surface area measurement of **biomaterials, drug carriers, and protein aggregates**.

---

## 2.4 Self-Assembly — Critical Packing Parameter

The **critical packing parameter (CPP)** predicts self-assembled morphology:

$$CPP = \frac{v}{a_0 \cdot l_c}$$

where $v$ = tail volume, $a_0$ = headgroup area, $l_c$ = tail length.

| CPP | Morphology |
|---|---|
| CPP < 1/3 | Spherical micelles |
| 1/3 < CPP < 1/2 | Cylindrical micelles |
| 1/2 < CPP < 1 | Bilayers / Vesicles |
| CPP ≈ 1 | Planar bilayers |
| CPP > 1 | Reverse (inverted) structures |

**Driving forces for self-assembly:**
1. **Hydrophobic effect** (dominant, $\sim 20$–$25$ $k_B T$ per CH₂ group transferred).
2. **van der Waals** between tails.
3. **Electrostatic repulsion** between headgroups.
4. **Hydrogen bonding** (e.g., in DNA duplexes, protein folding).

---

## 2.5 van der Waals Theory — Lifshitz Approach

For two semi-infinite slabs separated by a thin film of medium 3:

$$U_{vdW}(D) = -\frac{A_{132}}{12 \pi D^2} \quad \text{(per unit area)}$$

where $A_{132}$ is the **combined Hamaker constant** for media 1 and 2 interacting across medium 3:

$$A_{132} = A_{12} + A_{33} - A_{13} - A_{23}$$

**Microscopic (pairwise summation) vs. Macroscopic (Lifshitz) approaches:**

| Approach | Strengths | Limitations |
|---|---|---|
| Microscopic | Simple, intuitive | Ignores many-body effects |
| Lifshitz | Accounts for retardation, screening | Computationally intensive |

**Retardation Effect:**
At large separations ($D > \lambda/2\pi \approx 5$–$10$ nm), the electromagnetic correlations propagate at finite speed, reducing the vdW force. Retarded potential:

$$U_{ret} \propto \frac{1}{D^3} \quad \text{(instead of } 1/D^2\text{)}$$

---

## 2.6 Electrical Double Layers — Detailed Models

**Helmholtz Model:**

$$\sigma = \varepsilon \varepsilon_0 \frac{\psi_0}{d}, \quad C_H = \frac{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0}{d}$$

**Gouy–Chapman Solution (for small potential, $ze\psi \ll k_B T$):**

$$\psi(x) = \psi_0 \exp(-\kappa x)$$

$$\sigma = \varepsilon \varepsilon_0 \kappa \psi_0$$

**Stern Model (combination):**

$$\frac{1}{C_{total}} = \frac{1}{C_{Stern}} + \frac{1}{C_{Diffuse}}$$

**Surface Potential Decay (Gouy–Chapman exact solution):**

$$\tanh\left(\frac{ze\psi}{4k_B T}\right) = \tanh\left(\frac{ze\psi_0}{4k_B T}\right) \exp(-\kappa x)$$

---

## 2.7 DLVO — Energy Profile Analysis

**Total Interaction Energy (sphere-sphere, constant potential):**

$$U_{DLVO}(D) = -\frac{A R}{6D} + \frac{64 \pi R n_0 k_B T \gamma_0^2}{\kappa^2} \exp(-\kappa D)$$

**Energy Components:**
- $U_{vdW} \propto -1/D$ → strong short-range attraction
- $U_{EDL} \propto +\exp(-\kappa D)$ → screened long-range repulsion

**Critical Coagulation Concentration (CCC) — Schulze–Hardy Rule:**

$$CCC \propto \frac{1}{z^6}$$

for a symmetric $z:z$ electrolyte.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface Tension | $\gamma = dW/dA$ | $\gamma$ in N/m or J/m² |
| 2 | Young–Laplace (drop) | $\Delta P = 2\gamma / R$ | $R$ in m |
| 3 | Young–Laplace (bubble) | $\Delta P = 4\gamma / R$ | $R$ in m |
| 4 | Capillary Rise | $h = 2\gamma \cos\theta / (\rho g r)$ | $h$, $r$ in m |
| 5 | Young's Equation | $\gamma_{SV} = \gamma_{SL} + \gamma_{LV}\cos\theta$ | angles in rad |
| 6 | Work of Adhesion | $W_a = \gamma_{SV} + \gamma_{LV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | J/m² |
| 7 | Spreading Coefficient | $S = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL} - \gamma_{LV}$ | J/m² |
| 8 | Langmuir Isotherm | $\theta = KP / (1 + KP)$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | BET Isotherm | $\frac{P}{V(P_0 - P)} = \frac{1}{V_m C} + \frac{C-1}{V_m C} \cdot \frac{P}{P_0}$ | $V$ in mL/g |
| 10 | Gibbs Adsorption | $\Gamma = -\frac{C}{RT} \frac{d\gamma}{dC}$ | $\Gamma$ in mol/m² |
| 11 | Critical Packing Parameter | $CPP = v / (a_0 l_c)$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | vdW Sphere–Plane | $U = -AR / (6D)$ | $J$ |
| 13 | vdW Two Slabs (per area) | $U = -A / (12\pi D^2)$ | $J/m^2$ |
| 14 | Retarded vdW | $U \propto -B / D^3$ | $J$ |
| 15 | Debye Length | $\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 k_B T / (2 N_A e^2 I)}$ | m |
| 16 | Poisson–Boltzmann (1D) | $\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = \frac{2 n_0 z e}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0} \sinh\left(\frac{ze\psi}{k_B T}\right)$ | V/m² |
| 17 | Helmholtz Capacitance | $C_H = \varepsilon \varepsilon_0 / d$ | F/m² |
| 18 | Gouy–Chapman Surface Charge | $\sigma = \varepsilon \varepsilon_0 \kappa \psi_0$ | C/m² |
| 19 | Stern Combined Capacitance | $1/C_{tot} = 1/C_S + 1/C_D$ | 1/(F/m²) |
| 20 | DLVO (Sphere–Sphere) | $U = -AR/(6D) + \frac{64\pi R n_0 k_B T \gamma_0^2}{\kappa^2} e^{-\kappa D}$ | $J$ |
| 21 | Schulze–Hardy | $CCC \propto 1/z^6$ |  |
| 22 | CMC Free Energy | $\Delta G = RT \ln(\text{CMC})$ | J/mol |

> **Standard Constants (KEEP ON HAND):**
> $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹, $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\varepsilon_{water} \approx 78.5$ at 25°C, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Biophysical & Engineering Applications

| Concept | Application |
|---|---|
| Surface tension | Lung surfactant (alveolar stability), tear film, emulsions |
| Phase boundaries | Cell membrane integrity, biomaterial coatings |
| Adsorption isotherms | Protein adsorption on implants, drug loading in carriers |
| Self-assembly | Liposomes as drug delivery vehicles, block copolymer micelles |
| vdW forces | Gecko adhesion, AFM tip–surface interaction, colloidal stability |
| EDL | Electrophoresis, ion-exchange membranes, neural signaling |
| DLVO | Vaccine adjuvant design, biosensors, food colloids |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Young–Laplace Equation

**Goal:** Show that pressure difference across a curved spherical interface is $\Delta P = 2\gamma / R$.

**Step 1:** Consider a spherical droplet of radius $R$. Increase the radius by an infinitesimal amount $dR$ via a reversible, isothermal process.

**Step 2:** The surface area changes by:
$$dA = 4\pi(R + dR)^2 - 4\pi R^2 \approx 8\pi R \, dR \quad (\text{neglecting } (dR)^2)$$

**Step 3:** The work done against surface tension is:
$$dW_{surface} = \gamma \, dA = 8\pi \gamma R \, dR$$

**Step 4:** The work done expanding the droplet against the external pressure is:
$$dW_{pressure} = \Delta P \, dV = \Delta P \cdot 4\pi R^2 \, dR$$

**Step 5:** At equilibrium (no net energy change, or, equivalently, work done by pressure equals work against surface tension):
$$\Delta P \cdot 4\pi R^2 \, dR = 8\pi \gamma R \, dR$$

**Step 6:** Cancel $4\pi R \, dR$ from both sides:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{2\gamma}{R}}$$

For a soap bubble (two interfaces, inner and outer surfaces):
$$\Delta P = \frac{4\gamma}{R}$$

> This derivation is **examination-favorite** at KTU 2024. Memorize the cancellation step carefully.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm

**Assumptions:**
1. Fixed number of equivalent surface sites.
2. Monolayer coverage.
3. No interactions between adsorbed molecules.
4. Each site holds one molecule.

**Step 1:** Let $\theta$ be fractional surface coverage. Then uncovered fraction is $(1 - \theta)$.

**Step 2:** Rate of adsorption is proportional to pressure $P$ and fraction of bare sites:
$$r_{ads} = k_a (1 - \theta) P$$

**Step 3:** Rate of desorption is proportional to fraction of occupied sites:
$$r_{des} = k_d \theta$$

**Step 4:** At equilibrium, $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:
$$k_a (1 - \theta) P = k_d \theta$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\theta$:
$$k_a P - k_a P \theta = k_d \theta$$
$$k_a P = \theta (k_d + k_a P)$$
$$\theta = \frac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P} = \frac{KP}{1 + KP}$$

where $K = k_a / k_d$ is the **adsorption equilibrium constant** (dimensionless in this form, but can be expressed with units of pressure⁻¹).

**Step 6:** Rearrange for linear plot form (Langmuir plot of $P/\theta$ vs $P$):
$$\frac{P}{\theta} = \frac{1}{K} + P$$

> **KTU Tip:** The slope of $P/\theta$ vs $P$ should be **1** if the data obeys Langmuir; deviations indicate multilayer or heterogeneous adsorption.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Gibbs Adsorption Isotherm

**Goal:** Derive $\Gamma = -\frac{C}{RT} \frac{d\gamma}{dC}$ from thermodynamic principles.

**Step 1:** For a system with two components and a surface, the differential of surface free energy is:
$$dG = -S \, dT - P \, dV + \gamma \, dA + \mu_1 \, dn_1 + \mu_2 \, dn_2$$

**Step 2:** At constant $T$, $P$, $\mu$:
$$dG = \gamma \, dA$$

**Step 3:** Per unit area, with $n_1^S$, $n_2^S$ as surface excess moles:
$$d\gamma = -\Gamma_1 \, d\mu_1 - \Gamma_2 \, d\mu_2$$

**Step 4:** Choose the dividing surface such that $\Gamma_{solvent} = 0$. Then for the solute:
$$d\gamma = -\Gamma_2 \, d\mu_2$$

**Step 5:** For an ideal dilute solute, $d\mu_2 = RT \, d(\ln C) = (RT/C) \, dC$:
$$d\gamma = -\Gamma_2 \cdot \frac{RT}{C} \, dC$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange:
$$\Gamma_2 = -\frac{C}{RT} \frac{d\gamma}{dC}$$

For solutions where solute **lowers** $\gamma$ (surfactants), $d\gamma/dC < 0$, so $\Gamma > 0$ — positive adsorption at the surface. This is **why surfactants accumulate at interfaces**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Debye Length

**Step 1:** Start from Poisson's equation in 1D (planar geometry):
$$\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 2:** The charge density from Boltzmann distribution (for symmetric $z:z$ electrolyte):
$$\rho(x) = z e (n_+ - n_-) = z e \, n_0 \left[\exp\left(-\frac{ze\psi}{k_B T}\right) - \exp\left(+\frac{ze\psi}{k_B T}\right)\right]$$
$$\rho(x) = -2 n_0 z e \sinh\left(\frac{ze\psi}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into Poisson's equation:
$$\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = \frac{2 n_0 z e}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0} \sinh\left(\frac{ze\psi}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 4:** For small potentials ($ze\psi \ll k_B T$), use $\sinh(u) \approx u$:
$$\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} \approx \frac{2 n_0 z^2 e^2}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 k_B T} \psi$$

**Step 5:** Define the **Debye–Hückel parameter**:
$$\kappa^2 = \frac{2 n_0 z^2 e^2}{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 k_B T}$$

So the linearized equation becomes:
$$\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} = \kappa^2 \psi$$

**Step 6:** Apply boundary condition $\psi(0) = \psi_0$ and $\psi \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$:
$$\psi(x) = \psi_0 \exp(-\kappa x)$$

**Step 7:** Convert to ionic strength $I = \frac{1}{2} \sum c_i z_i^2$. For symmetric $z:z$ electrolyte with bulk concentration $c$ (mol/L), $I = z^2 c$ and $n_0 = N_A c$:

$$\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 k_B T}{2 N_A e^2 I}}$$

**Numerical check (physiological saline, $I = 0.15$ M, $T = 298$ K):**
$$\kappa^{-1} \approx \sqrt{\frac{78.5 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 298}{2 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2 \times 0.15}}$$

$$\kappa^{-1} \approx 7.83 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m} \approx 0.78 \, \text{nm}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation of DLVO Interaction Energy (Sphere–Sphere)

**Step 1:** Start with the **Derjaguin Approximation** (valid for $D \ll R$):
$$U_{sphere-sphere}(D) = \pi R \cdot U_{flat-flat}(D)$$

**Step 2:** vdW interaction per unit area between two flat plates:
$$U_{vdW,flat}(D) = -\frac{A_{132}}{12\pi D^2}$$

**Step 3:** Using Derjaguin approximation for sphere-sphere vdW:
$$U_{vdW}(D) = \pi R \cdot \left(-\frac{A_{132}}{12\pi D^2}\right) \cdot (1 \text{ in some derivations})$$

Standard form (sphere-sphere or sphere-plane):

$$U_{vdW}(D) = -\frac{A R}{6D}$$

**Step 4:** For EDL repulsion between two flat plates at constant surface potential $\psi_0$:
$$U_{EDL,flat}(D) = \frac{64 n_0 k_B T \gamma_0^2}{\kappa} \exp(-\kappa D)$$

where $\gamma_0 = \tanh\left(\frac{ze\psi_0}{4k_B T}\right)$.

**Step 5:** Apply Derjaguin approximation:
$$U_{EDL}(D) = \frac{64 \pi R n_0 k_B T \gamma_0^2}{\kappa^2} \exp(-\kappa D)$$

**Step 6:** Total DLVO energy:
$$U_{DLVO}(D) = -\frac{A R}{6D} + \frac{64 \pi R n_0 k_B T \gamma_0^2}{\kappa^2} \exp(-\kappa D)$$

**Step 7:** Conditions for stability:
- Stable colloid: $U_{max} \gg k_B T$ (energy barrier > ~10 $k_B T$).
- Coagulation: $U_{max} \ll k_B T$.

The **Schulze–Hardy rule** states that the critical coagulation ionic strength scales as:
$$I_{CCC} \propto \frac{1}{z^6}$$

for counter-ions of valence $z$.

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example — Capillary Rise (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** A glass capillary of radius $r = 0.5$ mm is dipped in water at 25°C ($\gamma = 72$ mN/m, $\theta = 0°$). Find the height of capillary rise. ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².)

**Solution:**

$$h = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

Step 1: Substitute $\gamma = 72 \times 10^{-3}$ N/m, $\cos\theta = 1$, $r = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

$$h = \frac{2 \times 72 \times 10^{-3} \times 1}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$h = \frac{144 \times 10^{-3}}{4.905}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 2.94 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m} = 2.94 \, \text{cm}}$$

> **Examination Tip:** Capillary rise is a **2-mark KTU favorite**. Units are critical — convert mm to m and mN/m to N/m before substituting.

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Debye Length in Saline

**Problem:** Calculate the Debye length for 0.1 M NaCl solution at 25°C. ($\varepsilon_r = 78.5$.)

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute ionic strength. For 0.1 M NaCl, $I = 0.1$ M (1:1 electrolyte, $z = 1$).

Step 2: Apply formula:
$$\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon \varepsilon_0 k_B T}{2 N_A e^2 I}}$$

Step 3: Substitute values (SI units):
- $\varepsilon = 78.5 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 6.95 \times 10^{-10}$ F/m
- $k_B T = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 298 = 4.116 \times 10^{-21}$ J
- $2 N_A e^2 I = 2 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19})^2 \times 0.1 = 3.09 \times 10^{-13}$ C²/(mol·m³)

Step 4: Numerator = $6.95 \times 10^{-10} \times 4.116 \times 10^{-21} = 2.86 \times 10^{-30}$

Step 5: $\kappa^{-1} = \sqrt{2.86 \times 10^{-30} / 3.09 \times 10^{-13}} = \sqrt{9.26 \times 10^{-18}}$

$$\boxed{\kappa^{-1} \approx 9.6 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{m} = 0.96 \, \text{nm}}$$

> **KTU Note:** As ionic strength **increases 10-fold**, $\kappa^{-1}$ **decreases 3.16-fold** ($\sqrt{10}$). Always remember this scaling.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Molecular Origin of Surface Tension

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Bulk Liquid Molecule] --> B[Surrounded by equal cohesive forces]
    B --> C[Net force = 0]
    D[Surface Molecule] --> E[Net inward pull from liquid side]
    E --> F[Reduced neighbors on vapor side]
    F --> G[Excess surface free energy gamma per unit area]
    G --> H[Surface minimizes area]
    H --> I[Spherical droplet geometry]
    I --> J[Young-Laplace pressure balance]
```

**Physical Interpretation:**
- A → B → C: The bulk molecule has no preferred direction of motion.
- D → E → F → G: A surface molecule experiences a net inward force → work is required to bring a molecule from bulk to surface → energy $\gamma$ per unit area is stored at the interface.
- G → H → I → J: To minimize this excess free energy, the surface area is minimized → spherical droplets (orbs) form. Curvature generates the Laplace pressure that drives capillary action.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Adsorption Isotherm Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Pressure or Concentration P] --> B{Type of Isotherm}
    B -->|Henry| C[Linear theta equals K P]
    B -->|Freundlich| D[Power law theta equals K P to the n]
    B -->|Langmuir| E[Monolayer saturation theta equals KP divided by 1 plus KP]
    B -->|BET| F[Multilayer with finite limit at P sub 0]
    C --> G[Low coverage regime]
    D --> H[Heterogeneous surface]
    E --> I[Identical sites no interaction]
    F --> J[Surface area measurement tool]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The central decision node $B$ classifies the isotherm by physical mechanism.
- Langmuir (E) is the most commonly tested in KTU 2024 biophysics — memorize the linear plot form $P/\theta = 1/K + P$.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Self-Assembly Pathway

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Amphiphile in Water] --> B{Concentration relative to CMC}
    B -->|Below CMC| C[Monomers dissolved freely]
    B -->|Above CMC| D[Spherical Micelles]
    D --> E[Increase tail volume or CPP]
    E --> F[Cylindrical Micelles]
    F --> G[Liquid Crystalline Phases]
    G --> H[Bilayers Vesicles Liposomes]
    H --> I[Inverted Hexagonal Phases]
    I --> J[Reverse Micelles in oil]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Below CMC (Critical Micelle Concentration), monomers exist freely.
- Above CMC, the system self-organizes to bury hydrophobic tails away from water.
- The morphology is dictated by the **critical packing parameter (CPP)**.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Electrical Double Layer Models (Evolution)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Charged Surface in Electrolyte] --> B[Helmholtz 1879]
    A --> C[Gouy Chapman 1910 1913]
    A --> D[Stern 1924]
    B --> B1[Rigid layer of counter-ions at distance d]
    B --> B2[Capacitor analogy]
    C --> C1[Diffuse Boltzmann distribution]
    C --> C2[Thermal motion competes with electrostatics]
    D --> D1[Compact Stern Layer]
    D --> D2[Diffuse Gouy Chapman Layer]
    D --> D3[Hybrid best physical model]
    B1 --> E[Modern EDL]
    C1 --> E
    D3 --> E
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Helmholtz (B):** Treats the EDL as a parallel-plate capacitor — too simple, ignores thermal motion.
- **Gouy–Chapman (C):** Adds thermal motion via Boltzmann distribution — but overestimates ion concentration near surface.
- **Stern (D):** Combines the best of both: a compact layer (Helmholtz) + a diffuse layer (Gouy–Chapman).
- Modern biophysics uses the **Stern model** for cell membranes and protein surfaces.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Block — DLVO Energy Profile

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Distance D between particles] --> B[U vdW attractive]
    A --> C[U EDL repulsive]
    B --> D[Negative 1 by D]
    C --> E[Positive exp negative kappa D]
    D --> F[Total DLVO Energy U]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Analyze U max}
    G -->|U max greater than 10 kT| H[Stable colloid]
    G -->|U max less than 5 kT| I[Coagulation]
    G -->|Secondary minimum| J[Reversible flocculation]
    H --> K[Energy barrier prevents sticking]
    I --> L[Particles aggregate irreversibly]
    J --> M[Weak aggregates redisperse easily]
```

**Interpretation:**
- $D$ is the surface–surface separation.
- $U_{vdW}$ is attractive (negative), scaling as $-1/D$ (or $-1/D^2$ for slabs).
- $U_{EDL}$ is repulsive (positive), scaling as $\exp(-\kappa D)$ — decays with Debye length.
- The total DLVO energy $U$ has two minima and a barrier.
- KTU problems often ask: "At what ionic strength does coagulation occur?" → find the condition $U_{max} \approx k_B T$.

---

## 4.6 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of Interfacial Biophysics

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Interfacial Biophysics System] --> B[Surface Phenomena]
    A --> C[Bulk Phase Interactions]
    A --> D[Transport Processes]
    B --> B1[Surface tension]
    B --> B2[Contact angle]
    B --> B3[Adsorption equilibria]
    C --> C1[van der Waals forces]
    C --> C2[Electrostatic interactions]
    C --> C3[Self-assembly]
    D --> D1[Diffusion]
    D --> D2[Capillary flow]
    D --> D3[Dynamics of EDL]
    B1 --> E[Stability of colloids membranes emulsions]
    B3 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Biomedical Applications]
    F --> F1[Drug delivery]
    F --> F2[Vaccine adjuvants]
    F --> F3[Biomaterials]
    F --> F4[Diagnostic biosensors]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The diagram maps the hierarchy: from individual phenomena (surface tension, vdW, EDL) up to macroscopic stability, then to real-world biomedical applications.
- KTU 2024 emphasizes connecting each theoretical concept to **biomedical / pharmaceutical applications**.
